content.text: Concurrent AI agents, streaming LLM responses, parallel tool calls, and background health checks all share a single operating system thread in Python's async runtime. The mechanism that makes this possible — scheduling thousands of concurrent operations without threads or processes — is the
. Understanding how the loop works, what blocks it, and how to keep it healthy is the foundation of writing reliable async agent systems.

is a scheduler that runs inside a single OS thread. It maintains a queue of ready coroutines and a set of pending I/O interests, then iterates in a tight cycle: run all ready callbacks, ask the OS which I/O descriptors are ready (via
), fire the callbacks for those descriptors, advance any expired timers, and repeat. Python's standard implementation lives in the
swap in a faster libuv-backed loop with the same interface.
function (a coroutine) yields control back to the loop whenever it hits an

expression. The loop records where to resume the coroutine once the awaited operation completes and immediately moves on to the next ready task. From the outside, thousands of coroutines appear to run simultaneously; internally, only one runs at a time, interleaved at every
The loop is not a background thread: it occupies the calling thread completely while running. Scripts create and destroy a loop with
. Long-running servers (FastAPI, aiohttp, Starlette) have their ASGI runner (uvicorn, Hypercorn) own the loop for the lifetime of the process.

Agent systems spend the overwhelming majority of their wall-clock time waiting: waiting for an LLM API to respond, waiting for a tool's HTTP endpoint, waiting for a database query, waiting for a vector search. Traditional multi-threaded designs handle this by blocking a thread per request — acceptable at tens of concurrent requests but expensive at thousands, because each thread consumes memory and scheduler overhead.

The event loop collapses all that waiting into a single thread. One agent worker can hold open thousands of in-flight LLM requests, process token stream chunks as they arrive, execute tool calls concurrently, and flush telemetry in the background — all with a single thread and near-zero coordination overhead.
The flip side is that any code that runs synchronously on the loop

for exactly as long as it runs. A 200 ms synchronous HTTP call, a blocking Redis client, or a slow JSON parse on a large LLM response pauses all other agent sessions on that worker. Understanding the loop is understanding why async discipline matters so much in production agent services.
— thousands of in-flight operations share one thread, avoiding the memory and scheduling overhead of one thread per request.
— because only one coroutine runs at a time, shared state (agent context, conversation history, metrics counters) is safe to read/write between await points without mutexes.
— the OS-level selector (epoll on Linux, kqueue on macOS, IOCP on Windows) is used directly, yielding near-optimal I/O throughput.
integrate directly into the scheduling cycle, making retry back-off, heartbeats, and step timeouts trivial to implement.
let coroutines be composed, raced, and cancelled with well-defined semantics.
— execution switches between coroutines only at explicit await points, making it much easier to reason about the order of events than with preemptive threads.

— computation-heavy steps (embedding large documents, parsing enormous JSON payloads, running a local model inference) hold the thread and freeze every concurrent agent session. Offloading to a thread or process pool adds complexity.
— one event loop uses one CPU core. Scaling to multiple cores requires multiple processes (e.g. uvicorn with multiple workers or multiprocessing).
— every caller of an async function must also be async (or explicitly bridge via
). Mixing sync and async code at boundaries requires care.
— unhandled exceptions in detached tasks (created with
without a reference) are silently dropped unless an exception handler is attached.
— only one event loop can run per OS thread. Calling
inside a running loop raises a RuntimeError; libraries must use await-compatible APIs throughout.
Each iteration of the event loop performs the following steps in order:
— any coroutine that was previously paused at an await point and whose awaitable has now completed is placed in the ready queue. The loop drains this queue completely before moving on.
— the loop calls the OS selector (
) with a timeout equal to the time until the nearest pending timer fires. The OS returns a list of file descriptors (sockets, pipes) that have data ready.
— for each ready descriptor, the loop calls the registered callback, which typically resumes a waiting coroutine.
are moved to the ready queue.
— the loop continues until it is stopped or until the top-level coroutine completes.

, the Python runtime suspends the coroutine and passes control back to the loop. The loop records the (future, coroutine) pair. When the future's result is set — typically by an I/O callback — the loop schedules the coroutine to resume by placing it in the ready queue. On the next iteration the coroutine picks up exactly where it left off, with the awaited value available.
This mechanism is built on Python generators:
functions compile to coroutine objects whose
method advances execution up to the next
uses this at its core, though the generator layer is hidden from application code.
The loop wraps a platform-specific selector:

on Windows. When an async socket send or receive is initiated, the loop registers the socket's file descriptor with the selector and suspends the calling coroutine. The selector efficiently monitors thousands of descriptors simultaneously using a kernel data structure (a red-black tree for epoll), so the wait costs essentially O(1) regardless of the number of monitored sockets.
wraps a coroutine in a Future-like object. Scheduling a task with
queues it for immediate execution in the current iteration. The task runs until its first
point, suspends, and the loop moves to the next ready task.
collects multiple tasks and returns a future that resolves when all of them complete, propagating the first exception unless

The diagram below shows the four major sources of work that feed the event loop — the ready queue, the OS I/O poller, the timer heap, and registered callbacks — and how the loop dispatches and re-registers work between each of them. The thread/process executor sits outside the loop thread and posts results back via thread-safe callbacks.

attrs.alt: asyncio event loop architecture — ready queue, I/O poller, timer heap, callbacks, and thread executor

content.text: Key insight from the diagram: the event loop never waits idly. If all coroutines are suspended on I/O, the loop blocks in
(zero CPU) until the OS signals a descriptor ready. Only then does it consume CPU again. This is why async I/O scales so efficiently.

Use an async event loop as the runtime for any component that is primarily I/O-bound: agent orchestrators that fan out multiple LLM or tool API calls, streaming response proxies, WebSocket servers for real-time token delivery, background task queues that poll databases, and any service where request concurrency is more important than raw CPU throughput.

use a single-threaded event loop for CPU-bound workloads such as local model inference, large embedding batch computation, or image/video processing. These tasks should run in a separate thread pool (
Use the event loop when operations spend >80% of time waiting for external responses.
Offload to executor when a single operation needs >5–10 ms of pure CPU time.
Use multiple processes (not the loop alone) when you need to utilize multiple CPU cores.
Let the ASGI framework (uvicorn, Hypercorn) own the loop in web services — do not create or close it manually.
The cleanest entry point for a standalone async agent script is
. It creates a new event loop, runs the top-level coroutine to completion, then closes the loop and cancels any lingering tasks.

import asyncio

async def run_agent(prompt: str) -> str:
    # Any async work here (LLM call, tool use, etc.)
    result = await call_llm(prompt)
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    answer = asyncio.run(run_agent("Summarise the news today"))
    print(answer)
to schedule a coroutine to run concurrently with the current one. Use
to fan out and collect results. Both patterns are fundamental to parallel tool calls.

import asyncio
from typing import Any

async def call_tool(name: str, args: dict) -> Any:
    # Simulate an async HTTP tool call
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
    return {"tool": name, "result": "ok"}

async def run_parallel_tools(tool_calls: list[dict]) -> list[Any]:
    tasks = [
        asyncio.create_task(call_tool(tc["name"], tc["args"]))
        for tc in tool_calls
    ]
    # gather preserves order; raises on first exception by default
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return list(results)

async def main():
    calls = [
        {"name": "web_search", "args": {"q": "asyncio"}},
        {"name": "calculator", "args": {"expr": "2+2"}},
        {"name": "read_file", "args": {"path": "/tmp/data.txt"}},
    ]
    results = await run_parallel_tools(calls)
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())
Step 3 — Background tasks with lifecycle management
Background tasks (heartbeats, metric flushes, run-status pollers) should be created with
and stored in a set. Attach a done callback to log exceptions and discard the reference, preventing silent failures and memory leaks.

import asyncio, logging

_background_tasks: set[asyncio.Task] = set()

def spawn(coro):
    """Schedule a fire-and-forget background task with exception logging."""
    task = asyncio.create_task(coro)
    _background_tasks.add(task)
    task.add_done_callback(_handle_done)

def _handle_done(task: asyncio.Task):
    _background_tasks.discard(task)
    if not task.cancelled():
        exc = task.exception()
        if exc:
            logging.error("Background task failed: %s", exc, exc_info=exc)

async def heartbeat(agent_id: str):
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(30)
        await post_heartbeat(agent_id)

async def main():
    spawn(heartbeat("agent-42"))
    await run_agent_session()   # main work
Blocking calls must never run directly on the loop thread. Use
for CPU-bound work that also bypasses the GIL.
Example 1 — Introductory: concurrent LLM calls with timeout
This example shows the most common async pattern in agent code: launching several coroutines concurrently and collecting results within a wall-clock timeout.

import asyncio, random

async def fake_llm(prompt: str, latency: float) -> str:
    """Simulate an LLM API call that takes `latency` seconds."""
    await asyncio.sleep(latency)
    return f"[response to '{prompt}']"

async def main():
    prompts = ["Translate to French", "Summarise", "Extract keywords"]
    latencies = [random.uniform(0.1, 0.5) for _ in prompts]

    # All three calls run concurrently; total wall time ≈ max(latencies)
    try:
        results = await asyncio.wait_for(
            asyncio.gather(
                *[fake_llm(p, l) for p, l in zip(prompts, latencies)]
            ),
            timeout=2.0,  # overall deadline
        )
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        print("Parallel LLM calls timed out")
        return

    for prompt, result in zip(prompts, results):
        print(f"{prompt}: {result}")

asyncio.run(main())
Example 2 — Realistic: async agent with streaming output and background heartbeat

A more production-like pattern: the agent streams token chunks to a client while a background task sends keep-alive pings, and a run-status poller notifies on completion. All three coroutines share the same event loop.

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import AsyncIterator

@dataclass
class AgentRun:
    run_id: str
    done: asyncio.Event = field(default_factory=asyncio.Event)
    result: str | None = None

async def stream_tokens(prompt: str) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
    """Simulate token-by-token streaming from an LLM."""
    words = f"Response for: {prompt}".split()
    for word in words:
        await asyncio.sleep(0.05)  # network latency between chunks
        yield word + " "

async def heartbeat_loop(run: AgentRun, interval: float = 5.0):
    """Send keep-alive pings until the run completes."""
    while not run.done.is_set():
        print(f"  [heartbeat] run={run.run_id} still active")
        try:
            await asyncio.wait_for(run.done.wait(), timeout=interval)
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            pass

async def status_poller(run: AgentRun):
    """Poll for completion every second (simulated)."""
    for _ in range(20):
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
        if run.done.is_set():
            print(f"  [poller] run={run.run_id} finished: {run.result}")
            return
    print(f"  [poller] timed out waiting for run={run.run_id}")

async def execute_run(run: AgentRun, prompt: str):
    # Stream tokens (simulates sending to client WebSocket)
    tokens = []
    async for token in stream_tokens(prompt):
        tokens.append(token)
        print(token, end="", flush=True)
    run.result = "".join(tokens).strip()
    run.done.set()

async def main():
    run = AgentRun(run_id="run-001")

    # Three concurrent coroutines on one event loop
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        tg.create_task(execute_run(run, "explain event loops"))
        tg.create_task(heartbeat_loop(run))
        tg.create_task(status_poller(run))

    print(f"\nFinal result: {run.result}")

asyncio.run(main())

Consider a production agent API built on FastAPI and uvicorn. Each HTTP request triggers an agent run that may span multiple LLM calls (planning, tool use, reflection) and several external tool HTTP calls, while the same process concurrently handles dozens of other active sessions.

# agent_api.py — simplified FastAPI agent endpoint
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio, httpx

app = FastAPI()
# Shared async HTTP client — reuses connection pool across all requests
http_client = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0)

async def call_tool_api(tool_name: str, payload: dict) -> dict:
    resp = await http_client.post(f"https://tools.internal/{tool_name}", json=payload)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

async def run_agent_stream(prompt: str):
    """Yield SSE chunks. Runs on the uvicorn event loop."""
    # Step 1: parallel planning + context retrieval
    plan, context = await asyncio.gather(
        call_tool_api("planner", {"prompt": prompt}),
        call_tool_api("retriever", {"query": prompt, "k": 5}),
    )
    yield f"data: {{'step':'planned'}}\n\n"

    # Step 2: stream LLM response
    async with http_client.stream("POST", "https://llm.internal/generate",
                                   json={"plan": plan, "context": context}) as r:
        async for chunk in r.aiter_text():
            yield f"data: {chunk}\n\n"

    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.post("/agent/run")
async def agent_run(request: Request):
    body = await request.json()
    return StreamingResponse(
        run_agent_stream(body["prompt"]),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
    )

# uvicorn agent_api:app --workers 4
# Each worker has its own event loop; the shared http_client is per-worker.
In this setup, uvicorn creates the event loop at worker startup. All requests share the same loop and the same
connection pool. If one request's LLM call takes 8 seconds, all other requests continue unaffected — the loop services their I/O callbacks while the slow one awaits. A synchronous
would block the loop for the full 8 seconds, stalling every other session.
Mistake 1 — Blocking the loop with synchronous calls

# WRONG — blocks the entire event loop for the duration of the HTTP call
import requests

async def fetch_tool_result(url: str) -> dict:
    resp = requests.get(url)      # sync! blocks the loop thread
    return resp.json()

# RIGHT — yields control during the network wait
import httpx

async def fetch_tool_result(url: str) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.get(url)
    return resp.json()
Mistake 2 — Calling asyncio.run() inside a running loop

# WRONG — raises RuntimeError: This event loop is already running
async def handler(request):
    result = asyncio.run(some_coroutine())   # nested run!
    return result

# RIGHT — just await the coroutine directly
async def handler(request):
    result = await some_coroutine()
    return result
Mistake 3 — Unrooted tasks (fire-and-forget without error handling)

# WRONG — exception is silently swallowed; task reference is garbage-collected
async def main():
    asyncio.create_task(might_fail())   # no reference stored!

# RIGHT — store the task reference and attach an error handler
_tasks: set = set()

def spawn_safe(coro):
    t = asyncio.create_task(coro)
    _tasks.add(t)
    t.add_done_callback(lambda t: (_tasks.discard(t),
                                    t.exception() and logging.error(t.exception())))

# WRONG — time.sleep blocks the OS thread, stalling all coroutines
async def retry_with_backoff():
    import time
    time.sleep(2)   # kills all concurrency for 2 seconds
    await do_retry()

# RIGHT
async def retry_with_backoff():
    await asyncio.sleep(2)   # suspends only this coroutine; loop continues
    await do_retry()
Never call blocking APIs on the loop thread
for thread-safe blocking I/O (file reads, sync DB drivers) and
Let the framework own the loop
. In uvicorn/FastAPI deployments, never call asyncio.run() or loop.run_until_complete() yourself — the server manages the loop lifecycle.
and attach done callbacks that log exceptions. Detached tasks whose exceptions go unlogged are debugging nightmares.
. It cancels all sibling tasks on the first failure, preventing orphaned tasks.
Set timeouts on every external call
or httpx/aiohttp per-request timeouts. An LLM API with no timeout can hold a coroutine slot open indefinitely.
. Instrument your service to measure the time between scheduling a callback and it actually running. Spikes indicate the loop is being blocked.
logs any callback that holds the loop for more than 50 ms.
per request wastes TCP connection setup time. Create one per process and share it.

A single asyncio event loop can sustain tens of thousands of concurrent I/O-bound operations with low CPU usage. Benchmarks with httpx show a single Python process handling 5,000–20,000 concurrent outbound HTTP requests limited by OS socket buffers and network bandwidth, not the event loop itself. For agent workloads making 5–20 parallel LLM/tool calls per session, a single uvicorn worker can often sustain hundreds of concurrent sessions.

Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) means only one thread runs Python bytecode at a time. For I/O-bound async code this is irrelevant — the loop spends most of its time in the C-level selector syscall, which releases the GIL. For CPU-bound work, the GIL prevents true parallelism even across threads.
bypasses the GIL by running in separate Python interpreter processes.

To scale across CPU cores, run multiple uvicorn workers (one event loop per process) behind an nginx or load balancer. Kubernetes horizontal pod autoscaling based on CPU or request rate is the standard production approach.

is a drop-in replacement for asyncio's default event loop, built on libuv (the C library powering Node.js). It typically delivers 2–4x higher throughput for network-heavy workloads by replacing the Python selector layer with a highly optimised C implementation. Install with

import uvloop
asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())
# Or, with uvicorn:
# uvicorn app:app --loop uvloop
Even in async code, large synchronous sections can cause head-of-line blocking. Decompose long chains of awaits with an occasional
to voluntarily yield control and let other tasks run, especially in loops that process large batches of items.

async def process_batch(items: list) -> list:
    results = []
    for i, item in enumerate(items):
        results.append(process_item(item))   # synchronous per-item work
        if i % 100 == 0:
            await asyncio.sleep(0)   # yield every 100 items
    return results
(stdlib) — the standard event loop, task scheduler, and I/O primitives. Use for all async coordination logic.
— C-backed event loop, 2–4x faster than asyncio's default. Recommended for production agent servers.
— async-first HTTP client with connection pooling, streaming, and timeout control. Preferred over aiohttp for new projects.
— mature async HTTP client/server; commonly found in existing agent codebases.
— async drivers for AWS, MySQL, and PostgreSQL respectively. Essential for non-blocking database access in agents.
— compatibility shim that runs the same async code on asyncio or Trio. FastAPI and Starlette use anyio internally.
— alternative async runtime with a stronger structured-concurrency model; simpler task cancellation semantics but smaller ecosystem.
— ASGI servers that manage the event loop for FastAPI / Starlette agent APIs.
backported to Python 3.10 for structured concurrency on older runtimes.
Monitoring the event loop in production:

# Detect slow callbacks: log any that hold the loop >50 ms
import asyncio, logging

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.slow_callback_duration = 0.05   # seconds
loop.set_debug(True)   # enable all asyncio debug warnings

# Prometheus / OpenTelemetry — measure event loop lag
import time

async def measure_loop_lag(histogram):
    while True:
        t0 = time.monotonic()
        await asyncio.sleep(0)   # yield and immediately reschedule
        lag = time.monotonic() - t0 - 0.0
        histogram.observe(lag)
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
The table below compares the asyncio event loop with other Python concurrency models relevant to building agent systems.
I/O-bound: LLM APIs, tool calls, streaming
Coroutines can be cancelled at any
into the coroutine. Agent code that holds resources (open files, DB transactions, lock-like state) must handle this with
to avoid leaking resources on cancellation.

async def agent_step_with_cleanup(run_id: str):
    lock_acquired = False
    try:
        await acquire_run_lock(run_id)
        lock_acquired = True
        result = await call_llm("next step")
        return result
    finally:
        # Always releases the lock, even on cancellation
        if lock_acquired:
            await release_run_lock(run_id)

An unhandled exception in a detached task does not propagate to the parent coroutine. In agent systems this means a crashing background poller will not terminate the main agent session — but it also means failures go unnoticed unless explicitly handled. Use
when you need exception propagation, and the spawn-with-callback pattern shown in the implementation section for true fire-and-forget tasks.

Every outbound call in an agent — LLM API, tool HTTP, database — must have an explicit timeout. Without one, a hanging upstream service holds a coroutine slot open indefinitely. Accumulate enough of these and the process appears to hang, accepting new connections but never responding. Wrap external calls in
with per-call deadlines, and configure separate connect and read timeouts on HTTP clients.

Graceful shutdown under SIGTERM requires flushing in-flight agent runs rather than hard-killing them. Register a signal handler that sets a shutdown event, waits for running tasks to complete (with a deadline), then stops the loop.

import asyncio, signal

async def serve(shutdown_event: asyncio.Event):
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGTERM,
                             lambda: shutdown_event.set())
    await shutdown_event.wait()
    # Drain active runs before exiting
    active = [t for t in asyncio.all_tasks() if t is not asyncio.current_task()]
    if active:
        await asyncio.wait(active, timeout=30)

Think of the event loop as an air-traffic controller at a single runway. Every coroutine is a plane wanting to use the runway to do some computation. When a plane needs to wait for fuel (I/O), the controller tells it to taxi to the apron and immediately clears the next plane for take-off. The controller never idles — as long as any plane is ready, it is landing or departing. The controller only waits when every single plane is sitting at the apron waiting for fuel.

The critical rule is that each plane must yield the runway voluntarily every time it encounters a wait. A plane that parks on the active runway (a blocking call) stops all other planes cold. The controller (the scheduler) cannot preempt it — there is only cooperative scheduling. Every
keyword is the pilot signalling "I'm parking; someone else can use the runway."
The event loop runs on one OS thread and interleaves coroutines at every await point, enabling thousands of concurrent I/O operations without threads.
Any synchronous code that runs without awaiting — blocking HTTP calls, time.sleep(), heavy CPU work — stalls all other coroutines for its entire duration.
Use asyncio.to_thread() for blocking I/O libraries and ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU-bound work; never run these directly on the loop thread.
Let the ASGI server (uvicorn, Hypercorn) own the event loop in web services; call asyncio.run() only at the top-level entry point of scripts.
Store every create_task() reference and attach a done callback that logs exceptions; detached tasks silently swallow errors.
Set explicit timeouts on every outbound call (LLM API, tool HTTP, DB) to prevent coroutine slot exhaustion from hanging upstreams.
uvloop replaces the default asyncio selector with a libuv-backed implementation, delivering 2-4x throughput gains in network-heavy agent servers with zero code changes.
asyncio.TaskGroup (Python 3.11+) provides structured concurrency: if any child task raises, all siblings are cancelled automatically, preventing orphaned background work.
